guidelines.title: StorageAdapter Protocol is the single storage abstraction
guidelines.content: All data access goes through StorageAdapter Protocol (core/storage.py). PostgreSQLAdapter MUST implement the same 6 methods as JSONFileStorage: load_data, save_data, exists, list_projects, load_global, save_global. No module should access storage directly — always through the adapter. For new entity types, add to EntityType enum and DEFAULT_STRUCTURES.
guidelines.rationale: Phase 1 lesson L-001: Protocol + @runtime_checkable made migration mechanical. Same principle applies to PostgreSQL adapter. Structural subtyping means any class with the right methods satisfies the Protocol.

guidelines.title: Every LLM interaction goes through an LLMContract
guidelines.content: No ad-hoc prompting. Every LLM call MUST use a registered contract from core/llm/contract.py with: input_schema, output_schema, system_prompt_template, validation_rules, retry_strategy. Contracts are versioned (e.g., 'knowledge-suggest-v1'). Provider capabilities checked before execution — fallback if JSON mode not supported.
guidelines.rationale: AD-3 from architecture doc. Contracts make LLM interactions auditable, reproducible, and testable. Without contracts, prompt quality degrades silently.

guidelines.title: API endpoints follow RESTful conventions from Section 8.1
guidelines.content: Base path: /api/v1. Resource naming: plural nouns (tasks, decisions, knowledge). Nested under /projects/{slug}/. Standard verbs: GET (list/get), POST (create), PATCH (update), DELETE (remove). Return 201 on create, 200 on success, 404 on not found, 422 on validation error. All responses include JSON body with entity data.
guidelines.rationale: Consistent API design makes the frontend predictable and the CLI remote mode straightforward to implement.

guidelines.title: Use _build_task_entry pattern — single entry builder per entity
guidelines.content: When multiple code paths create the same entity (e.g., cmd_add_tasks and cmd_approve_plan both create task entries), extract a single builder function (_build_task_entry). NEVER duplicate entry construction logic. When adding a field to any entity, use the 8-location checklist: (1) add contract optional, (2) add contract types, (3) add contract invariant_texts, (4) update contract optional, (5) update contract types, (6) cmd_add builder, (7) cmd_update updatable, (8) cmd_show display.
guidelines.rationale: Phase 1 lessons L-002 (8-location checklist) and L-003 (hidden 9th location in approve_plan). The _build_task_entry refactoring eliminated this entire class of bugs.
examples:
def _build_task_entry(t: dict, source_idea_id: str = None) -> dict:
    # Single source of truth for task schema

guidelines.title: Deep-verify every non-trivial task
guidelines.content: After implementing any task with significant logic, run multi-agent adversarial verification (deep-verify) before marking DONE. Use 2-3 parallel agents with non-overlapping scopes (e.g., data flow, spec compliance, API/imports). Phase 1 deep-verify caught 9 real bugs across 4 tasks that passed acceptance criteria and manual review.
guidelines.rationale: Phase 1 lessons L-007 (deep-verify catches bugs AC misses) and L-010 (parallel agents with non-overlapping scopes). Cost of deep-verify is low vs cost of bugs reaching production.

guidelines.title: PostgreSQL schema matches Section 7.4 conceptual design
guidelines.content: Use the schema from FORGE-PLATFORM-V2.md Section 7.4 as starting point. Key tables: projects, objectives, key_results, ideas, tasks, decisions, changes, lessons, guidelines, knowledge, knowledge_versions, ac_templates. Junction tables for many-to-many: knowledge_links, task_ac, task_dependencies, task_knowledge, idea_relations. Use GIN indexes for array columns (scopes, tags). Add FTS index on knowledge.
guidelines.rationale: AD-1: PostgreSQL chosen for relational model fitting entity relationships. Schema already designed and verified in architecture doc.
examples:
CREATE TABLE knowledge (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, project_id INT, ext_id TEXT, title TEXT, category TEXT, current_version INT, status TEXT, scopes TEXT[], tags TEXT[]);
CREATE INDEX idx_knowledge_scopes ON knowledge USING GIN (scopes);

guidelines.title: Next.js App Router with Zustand stores
guidelines.content: Use Next.js 14 App Router (app/ directory). State management via Zustand (projectStore, entityStore, executionStore). API calls through a typed API client (lib/api.ts). WebSocket connection via custom useWebSocket hook. Component structure: pages in app/, reusable components in components/, DnD components in components/dnd/, entity cards in components/entities/.
guidelines.rationale: AD-9 and architecture doc Section 9.1. Zustand is simpler than Redux for our use case. App Router provides server components for initial data loading.

guidelines.title: DnD Kit for drag & drop interactions
guidelines.content: Use @dnd-kit/core for drag & drop. Follow the DnD interaction matrix from Section 9.2: entity-to-entity (knowledge→task = link), entity-to-zone (lesson→'Create Knowledge' = promote), entity-to-entity (task→task = dependency). Each draggable item is a DragItem component, each target is a DropZone. RelationshipBuilder component handles the creation of links/relationships after drop.
guidelines.rationale: AD-9: DnD Kit chosen over deprecated react-beautiful-dnd. Flexible enough for entity-to-entity interactions, not just list reordering.

guidelines.title: Event sourcing via Redis Streams for platform mode
guidelines.content: Write path: API validates → emit event to Redis Stream → consumers project to PostgreSQL. Read path: query PostgreSQL (projected state). Event types: task.status_changed, knowledge.updated, decision.created, etc. (12 types from Section 8.2). WebSocket broadcaster consumes events and pushes to connected clients. JSON standalone mode has NO event sourcing — direct CRUD only.
guidelines.rationale: AD-7: Event sourcing with Redis Streams gives full audit trail, replay, and natural real-time events. Feature parity matrix (Section 7.2) makes the boundary clear.

guidelines.title: Idempotent guards on all entity creation operations
guidelines.content: Any operation that creates one entity from another needs TWO guards: (1) source already-processed check (e.g., lesson.promoted_to_knowledge), (2) target dedup check (e.g., knowledge with same category+title). API endpoints that create entities must be idempotent — POST with same data twice should not create duplicates.
guidelines.rationale: Phase 1 lesson L-008: deep-verify caught duplicate creation in promote operations. API retries make this even more critical.

guidelines.title: Docker Compose for local platform development
guidelines.content: Four services: forge-api (FastAPI, port 8000), forge-web (Next.js, port 3000), postgres (PostgreSQL 16, port 5432), redis (Redis 7, port 6379). Use volumes for data persistence. Environment variables for configuration. Health checks for service dependencies. Based on Section 10.2.
guidelines.rationale: Section 10.1 service architecture. Docker Compose is the simplest way to run the full platform locally.

guidelines.title: Snapshot timing — capture values BEFORE modifying phases
guidelines.content: When a threshold depends on an 'original' value in multi-phase processing, capture it BEFORE any phase that modifies it. Place threshold checks BEFORE any early returns that would skip them. This applies to context assembly budget, API rate limiting, pagination offsets, and any pipeline with sequential phases.
guidelines.rationale: Phase 1 lesson L-004: context.py budget system had snapshot timing bug where original_total was captured AFTER Phase 2 capping, and 80% check was inside Phase 3 loop that only runs above 100%.

guidelines.title: SKILLs must be created using Anthropic skill-creator framework
guidelines.content: All custom skills in the platform MUST follow the Anthropic skill-creator framework (https://github.com/anthropics/skills/tree/main/skills/skill-creator). Each skill MUST have: (1) a SKILL.md with YAML frontmatter (name, description) and markdown instructions, (2) optional bundled resources in scripts/, references/, assets/ subdirectories. SKILL.md should stay under 500 lines with progressive disclosure — reference bundled resources for details. Use imperative voice, explain the 'why', and follow the Lack of Surprise principle. Test cases go to evals/evals.json with realistic 2-3 user prompts per skill.
guidelines.rationale: Standardizing on the Anthropic skill-creator framework ensures consistent skill structure, testability via built-in eval tooling, and compatibility with Claude Code / Claude.ai / Cowork platforms. The framework provides grading, benchmarking, and iteration workflows out of the box.
examples:
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md          # Required: frontmatter + instructions
├── scripts/          # Optional: executable code
├── references/       # Optional: documentation
├── assets/           # Optional: templates, icons
└── evals/
    └── evals.json    # Test cases

guidelines.title: Main menu must include full Skills management UI
guidelines.content: The platform main menu/navigation MUST include a 'Skills' section with full CRUD capabilities: (1) Add/create new skill — form with name, description, category, SKILL.md content editor, resource upload, (2) List/browse skills — filterable, searchable grid/list view, (3) View skill details — full SKILL.md preview, metadata, usage stats, linked evals, (4) Edit skill — inline editing of SKILL.md and metadata, (5) Delete skill — with confirmation dialog and dependency check (warn if skill is used by active tasks). The Skills section should be a top-level navigation item, not buried in settings.
guidelines.rationale: Skills are a core concept in Forge — they define structured execution patterns for tasks. Making them first-class citizens in the UI ensures discoverability, encourages reuse, and allows non-CLI users to manage them effectively.

guidelines.title: Skills must support status lifecycle: DRAFT → ACTIVE → DEPRECATED
guidelines.content: Every skill has a status field with lifecycle: DRAFT → ACTIVE → DEPRECATED → ARCHIVED. (1) DRAFT — skill is being developed, visible only in management UI, cannot be assigned to tasks. (2) ACTIVE — skill is promoted and ready for use, appears in task assignment dropdowns and skill picker. (3) DEPRECATED — skill is marked as outdated, existing task assignments still work but new assignments show a warning. (4) ARCHIVED — skill is hidden from all views except admin. Promotion from DRAFT to ACTIVE requires: valid SKILL.md with frontmatter, at least one eval test case, TESLint pass (0 errors), and explicit user action ('Promote' button). If TESLint reports errors, user CAN force-override the block ('Promote anyway' button with confirmation dialog). Force-promoted skills are flagged (promoted_with_warnings: true) and display a warning badge alongside ACTIVE status. Promotion history records: who, when, error/warning counts, whether force override was used. The UI must clearly distinguish DRAFT vs ACTIVE vs ACTIVE+warnings vs DEPRECATED skills.
guidelines.rationale: Not all skills are ready for production use. A clear status lifecycle prevents half-baked skills from being used in real tasks while encouraging iterative development. The promotion gate (valid SKILL.md + evals + TESLint) ensures minimum quality, while the force override gives experienced users flexibility when they know errors are acceptable (e.g., intentionally disabled rules).
examples: Skill card shows: [DRAFT] badge in orange, [ACTIVE] badge in green, [ACTIVE ⚠] badge in green+yellow triangle (force-promoted with errors), [DEPRECATED] badge in gray

guidelines.title: Skills must support categorization and grouping
guidelines.content: Skills MUST have: (1) category — primary classification: workflow, analysis, generation, validation, integration, refactoring, testing, deployment, documentation, custom. (2) tags — free-form labels for cross-cutting concerns (e.g., 'backend', 'security', 'ai'). (3) scopes — alignment with guideline scopes for automatic context loading. The UI must support: (a) browsing skills by category (sidebar filter or tab groups), (b) filtering by tags and status, (c) search by name/description, (d) grouping view toggle (by category, by status, by scope, flat list). Skills API endpoints: GET /api/v1/skills?category=X&status=ACTIVE&tags=backend&scope=api for filtered queries.
guidelines.rationale: As the number of skills grows, findability becomes critical. Categorization ensures users can discover relevant skills quickly. Scope alignment enables automatic skill suggestion when creating tasks in a given domain.
examples:
Category groups in UI:
  Workflow: [next, plan, do, run]
  Analysis: [discover, review, compound]
  Validation: [deep-verify, gates-check]
  Integration: [onboard, import-guidelines]

guidelines.title: AI annotations for all interactive UI elements
guidelines.content: Every interactive UI element (buttons, forms, filters, editable fields, lists, action menus, toggles) MUST have an AI annotation via useAIElement() or useAIPage() that registers it in the page context. Each annotation MUST include: (1) a stable id, (2) element type (button/form/filter/list/input/display/section/action), (3) human-readable label, (4) current value for stateful elements, (5) actions[] array defining available operations with toolName, toolParams, description, and availability conditions. This ensures the AI assistant can see what the user sees and knows exactly which tools to call and with what parameters to execute any action on the page. Pages without annotations are invisible to the AI — treat missing annotations as a bug.
guidelines.rationale: The AI sidebar reads page context from annotations to understand what the user is looking at and what actions are available. Without annotations, the AI cannot see the page content, cannot suggest relevant actions, and cannot execute operations on behalf of the user. Every actionable element needs a contract so the AI knows HOW to interact with it.

guidelines.title: No direct artifact writes by LLM
guidelines.content: LLM agents must NEVER write files directly to forge_output/. All data persistence must go through core Python modules (core.pipeline, core.decisions, core.research, core.changes, etc.) using their CLI interfaces. Research files are written via the content field in core.research add. Draft plans are stored in tracker.json via core.pipeline draft-plan.
guidelines.rationale: Ensures data integrity, contract validation, dedup, and auditability. Direct writes bypass validation and can create orphan files that drift from tracked state.
